# Forgotten Color Theorist — Amy Sawyer

**Source:** [Color Nerd](https://www.youtube.com/@ColorNerd1) (YouTube Shorts)
**Date:** 2025-02-04
**URL:** https://www.youtube.com/shorts/uwauAiE51ss
**Views:** 5,881

## Description

Amy Sawyer (1863–1945) — painter, illustrator, and color theorist who patented a color wheel in 1911 with primaries closer to CMY than traditional RYB.

## Transcript

Here's an artist for you to check out — Amy Sawyer. She was active at the end of the 18th and beginning of the 19th century. She synthesized academic style with the techniques and color theory of Impressionism and Post-Impressionism, and painted things like this, made multimedia collage pieces like this.

Here's a review of one of her decorative panels which kind of gives it a backhanded compliment, saying her colors are "sumptuously harmonious" — and yet it says it just amounts to a nice piece of decoration.

She was in fact a color theorist in her own right and actually applied for a patent after she retired from painting for a color wheel of her own design that listed as the primary colors: primrose, rose, and turquoise — which are closer to cyan, cool yellow, and magenta.

## Key Facts

- **Amy Sawyer** (1863–1945) — painter, illustrator, playwright; based in Ditchling, Sussex
- Active ~1887–1909/1913; retired from painting due to lead poisoning
- Synthesized academic painting with Impressionist/Post-Impressionist color theory
- Described as having "sumptuously harmonious" colors
- **Patented a color wheel (~1911)** with primaries:
  - **Primrose** (≈ cool yellow)
  - **Rose** (≈ magenta)
  - **Turquoise** (≈ cyan)
- Her primaries were essentially CMY — remarkably ahead of the dominant RYB model of her era
